Yes, it is possible to run a different `create_app` function or pass parameters to `create_app` when running a CLI command without explicitly setting an environment variable. This can be achieved by leveraging the Flask application context and the CLI command lifecycle. When you define a CLI command using `@app.cli.command`, it is executed within the context of the Flask application. This means that the CLI command has access to the application instance and its configuration. 

You can customize the `create_app` function based on the context in which it is being used. For example, you can check if the application is being run as a CLI command by inspecting the `sys.argv` or using a flag that is set internally. This approach allows you to differentiate between running the app as a server and running a CLI command. 

One way to do this is by using a flag or a flag-like variable that is set when the CLI command is executed. This flag can be used to determine whether the `create_app` function should be called with different parameters. This avoids the need for an environment variable and makes the CLI command more user-friendly. 

You can also pass parameters directly to the `create_app` function by using a custom configuration or by modifying the `create_app` function to accept additional arguments. This allows you to have more control over the application's behavior when it is run in different contexts. 

By using the `app` object in the CLI command, you can access the configuration and other attributes of the Flask application. This gives you the flexibility to modify the behavior of the application based on the context in which it is being used. 

For instance, if you're running a CLI command, you can check if the application is in a CLI context by examining the `sys.argv` or using a custom flag that is set during the CLI command execution. This allows you to run a different `create_app` function or pass different parameters to it. 

This approach eliminates the need for environment variables and makes your CLI commands more intuitive. Instead of requiring users to set environment variables, you can simply run `flask my_command` and have the application handle the context internally. 

You can also use the `app` object to determine if the application is running as a CLI command or as a server. This is particularly useful if you want to load different configurations or initialize different components based on the context.
